title: Inserting from a Register
subtitle: {key:Ctrl-R} is paste — but with a twist.
summary: {key:Ctrl-R}{reg} pastes a register contents from inside *insert mode*. {key:Ctrl-R} {key:Ctrl-O} pastes literally (no formatting); {key:Ctrl-R} {key:Ctrl-P} pastes and re-indents.

blocks.text: {key:Ctrl-R}{reg} from *insert mode* pastes the contents of any register. Unlike Normal-mode {key:p}, this works *inline*: characters appear at the cursor as if you'd typed them.

sequence.note: Vim shows a `"` prompt, waits for register name

rows: Insert register a *literally* (no autoindent, no abbreviations)
Evaluate an expression, insert the result

blocks.title: What Does Each Variant Do
blocks.text:
Plain {key:Ctrl-R}{reg} processes the inserted text as if you'd typed it: special characters like {key:<CR>} or {key:<Tab>} act like real keypresses, autoindent triggers, abbreviations expand. That's good for plain text; bad if your register contains code with leading whitespace.

{key:Ctrl-R}{key:Ctrl-O}{reg} is the literal version — paste exactly the bytes, no interpretation. Use this for code with significant indentation.

{key:Ctrl-R}{key:Ctrl-P}{reg} pastes and re-indents per current `'indentexpr'`. Useful for moving code between contexts.

{key:Ctrl-R}{key:=} opens the *expression register* prompt — type any Vim expression (math, function call, variable) and the result is inserted. {key:Ctrl-R}{key:=}{key:5}{key:*}{key:7}{key:Enter} inserts `35`.
{key:Ctrl-R}{key:"} pastes the unnamed register. {key:Ctrl-R}{key:0} pastes the last yank. {key:Ctrl-R}{key:+} pastes the system clipboard.
